nativeRange.habitat: Coral and rocky reef slopes, reef flats, and shallow seamounts; typically encountered between roughly 3–30 m but recorded as deep as 250 m. Often seen in midwater schools above the reef, retreating to the reef structure at night.

decorPreferences: live rock with crevices for nighttime shelter

diet.notes: In the wild, the Lemon Butterflyfish feeds primarily on zooplankton picked from the water column above the reef, rounding out its diet with benthic invertebrates, small crustaceans, and algae. It has also been documented opportunistically eating the egg clusters of damselfishes, and cleaning behavior toward other fish has been observed. In captivity it generally accepts a varied diet of finely chopped marine fish, crustacean meat, mysis shrimp, and frozen carnivore preparations. Offering several small meals per day is commonly advised, and supplementing with a vitamin or color-enhancing formula is widely recommended to help maintain the species' bright yellow coloration.

avoidWith: other butterflyfish unless added together to a large tank

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Thomas Irvine, 2048x1463. Tack-sharp, highly vibrant profile on a deep-blue background; clearly shows lemon-yellow body, vertical rows of dark spots, black eye bar, and black caudal-base spot. Most striking of the pool.
Photo by Krinkle, 3867x2900. Sharp full-body lateral view against reef; all diagnostic markings visible. Aquarium setting.
Photo by cello, 2048x1365. In-situ wild shot over reef showing natural behavior; whole fish in focus with diagnostic features, slight greenish water cast.

summary: The Lemon Butterflyfish (Chaetodon miliaris) is a bright lemon-yellow Hawaiian endemic marked by vertical rows of small dark spots, a black eye bar, and a black spot at the base of the tail. It is widely regarded as one of the hardier and more approachable butterflyfishes, though it is not reef-safe.
careNotes:
The Lemon Butterflyfish is largely endemic to the Hawaiian Archipelago, with its range extending to Johnston Atoll and the Midway Islands. Unlike many members of the genus Chaetodon that form strict monogamous pairs and defend feeding territories, this species tends to gather in midwater schools above shallow reef slopes, where it picks zooplankton from the water column. At night, individuals retreat into the reef structure to shelter. This schooling tendency means the Lemon Butterflyfish can be kept singly, as a pair, or in a small group — provided all individuals are introduced to a sufficiently large aquarium at the same time — which is an uncommon degree of flexibility for the group.

It is widely regarded as one of the hardier butterflyfishes and is commonly recommended as an entry point into the family for aquarists new to the group. That said, it shares the standard butterflyfish caveat: it is not reef-safe. It has been documented nipping at LPS and soft coral polyps (the fleshy, tentacle-bearing tissue of large-polyp stony corals), soft corals, anemones, tubeworms, and ornamental shrimp. A fish-only or FOWLR (fish-only with live rock) aquarium is generally advised. A minimum footprint of around 150 cm and a volume of at least 500 liters is recommended to give this active midwater swimmer adequate open space. Most individuals are reported to accept prepared frozen foods readily upon arrival, which is a notable advantage compared to many of its relatives.
breedingNotes: The Lemon Butterflyfish is oviparous (egg-laying), producing pelagic larvae that drift in open water. Pairing behavior has been observed during breeding in the wild, though the species is less strictly monogamous than many other Chaetodon. Successful captive breeding has not been documented in the aquarium hobby, and rearing the free-floating larvae is generally regarded as impractical for home aquarists.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Provided minimum tank size (70 gal), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), specific gravity (1.020–1.025), care level (Moderate), temperament (Peaceful), reef-safe status (no), and diet/feeding guidance.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name (Chaetodon miliaris Quoy & Gaimard, 1825), family Chaetodontidae, order Acanthuriformes, max length 14.9 cm SL, native range (Hawaii and Johnston Island), depth 0–250 m, oviparous reproduction with pairing during breeding, and schooling/zooplanktivore behavior. Environmental temperature range 15.3–21.7°C reflects the species' depth range and was not used as the captive-keeping range.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (13–14 cm), minimum tank size (~700 L), temperature (22–27°C), peaceful temperament, and not-reef-safe status; also confirmed broader native range including Midway Islands and stray records from Taiwan.

additional.name: Waikīkī Aquarium species guide
additional.notes: Authoritative regional source from the species' native range; confirmed maximum size 6.5 in (16.5 cm), depth to 250 m, Hawaiian endemism, midwater schooling behavior above reef slopes, zooplankton diet, cleaning behavior, and opportunistic damselfish-egg predation. Provided the Hawaiian common name lau wiliwili.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic larvae; captive breeding has not been documented in the aquarium hobby literature reviewed.
